title: What is XOR?
concept: XOR ("exclusive or") is a logic gate that outputs true only when its two inputs are different — one on, one off — and false whenever they match.

exercises.prompt: Recap: A perceptron learns an OR gate by outputting true whenever...

options.text: at least one input is on

exercises.explain: Right! OR only needs ONE input to be on to fire true — that's exactly the kind of pattern a single perceptron can learn.

cards.title: The hallway light trick
cards.body: Ever notice a hallway with a light switch at BOTH ends? Flip either switch and the light flips too. The light is ON only when the two switches are in different positions — flip the same one back and they match again, and the light goes OFF. You've been living inside a logic gate this whole time.

cards.title: What is XOR?
cards.body: XOR ("exclusive or") is a logic gate that outputs true only when its two inputs are different — one ON, one OFF. If the inputs match (both ON or both OFF), XOR outputs false.

cards.title: Odd sock day
cards.body: Imagine you grab two socks in the dark. If they're different colors, you've got an odd pair — that's a "true" for odd-sock day! If they match, nothing special happens — that's "false." XOR only celebrates the mismatch.

cards.title: Walking the truth table
cards.body:
Let's check every combination for inputs A and B:
A=0, B=0 → 0 (both off, they match)
A=0, B=1 → 1 (different!)
A=1, B=0 → 1 (different!)
A=1, B=1 → 0 (both on, they match)
Only the "different" rows fire true.

cards.title: XOR isn't just OR
cards.body: It's easy to mix these up. Plain OR says "true if ANY input is on" — including when BOTH are on. XOR is stricter: it says "true only if EXACTLY ONE is on." The both-true case is exactly where they disagree.

cards.title: OR vs. XOR
cards.body: Same two inputs, one key difference:

left.text:
True if ANY input is on
(1,1) still counts as true

right.text:
True only if inputs DIFFER
(1,1) flips back to false

exercises.prompt: Two hallway switches start in the SAME position (both up). The light is currently OFF. What happens if you flip just ONE switch?
exercises.explain: Flipping one switch makes the two positions DIFFERENT — and XOR turns true when inputs differ.

exercises.prompt: True or false: For XOR, the input pair (1, 1) — both inputs on — produces a true output.
exercises.explain: False! When both inputs match (both on), XOR outputs false. XOR only fires when the inputs are different.

exercises.sentence: XOR outputs true only when its two inputs ___.
exercises.explain: XOR is the 'exclusive' or — it fires only when the inputs are different from each other.

exercises.prompt: Which input pair makes an XOR gate output TRUE?
exercises.explain: (0,1) is the only mismatched pair here — exactly what XOR is looking for.

exercises.prompt: A security system arms itself using two sensors — a door sensor and a window sensor — wired as XOR. It should alert ONLY if exactly one is triggered, not if both are shut, and not even if both are somehow triggered at once. If the door sensor reads 1 (open) and the window sensor reads 1 (open), what does the XOR alarm output, and why?

options.text: 0 — both inputs match (both are 1), so XOR sees no difference to flag
1 — because at least one sensor is triggered
1 — because two triggers means double trouble
It's undefined — XOR can't handle two 1s

exercises.explain: Correct! XOR cares about DIFFERENCE, not quantity. Two matching 1s look just like two matching 0s to XOR: false either way. Next up: could the AND/OR perceptron you just met actually learn this pattern?
